title: Agentic Research Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for scientific-research agents — literature review, hypothesis generation, experimental design, and running experiments through lab automation — where the high-stakes action is a physical experiment that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or carries biosafety implications. It adds a gate the rest of the series doesn't have: a wet-lab boundary. The agent proposes; a human authorizes anything physical. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds the things that make research distinct: grounding in primary literature with provenance for every claim, a deterministic protocol validator with dual-use/biosafety screening the model cannot override, reproducibility as a first-class property, and human authorization before any physical run.

nodes.label: Principal Researcher
nodes.description: Scientist who reviews hypotheses and authorizes any physical experiment; the accountable decision-maker.

nodes.label: Biosafety Officer
nodes.description: Authorizes protocols with biosafety / dual-use implications before any wet-lab run.

nodes.label: Research Goal
nodes.description: The question or objective: a hypothesis to test, a literature question, a protocol to run.

nodes.label: Research API
nodes.description: Authenticates the request, resolves lab/tenant scope, routes to the pipeline.

nodes.label: Research Pipeline
nodes.description: Deterministic DAG: retrieve -> hypothesize -> design protocol -> validate -> authorize -> run.

nodes.label: Hypothesis / Design Agent
nodes.description: LLM in a bounded node: reviews literature, forms hypotheses, and drafts an experimental protocol with citations.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Literature Index
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: primary scientific literature and prior experimental data.

nodes.label: Experimental Data Store
nodes.description: Raw and processed results, versioned, with the run that produced each datum.

nodes.label: Protocol Validator
nodes.description: Deterministic checks: protocol well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety rules the model cannot override.

nodes.label: Experiment Authorization Gate
nodes.description: Routes any physical action (reagents, lab robot, biosafety) to human authorization; dry-run vs wet-lab tier.

nodes.label: Lab Automation
nodes.description: Robotic instruments and reagent handling — the physical actuation surface.

nodes.label: Provenance Store
nodes.description: Maps every claim/finding to its source literature and to the run that produced its data (reproducibility).

nodes.label: Policy & Safety
nodes.description: Tenant isolation, data governance, and screening of untrusted document/data input.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access; binds a wet-lab authorization to a researcher / biosafety principal.

nodes.label: Audit / Lab Notebook
nodes.description: Append-only record: sources, hypotheses, protocols, validations, authorizations, runs, results.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, reproducibility and claim-grounding metrics, eval vs replicated results.

functional: Review primary literature and prior data to inform hypotheses.
Generate hypotheses and draft experimental protocols, with citations.
Validate a protocol deterministically before any physical action.
Require human authorization for any experiment that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or has biosafety implications.
Run authorized protocols through lab automation and capture results.
nonFunctional: Reproducibility is first-class: every claim and finding traces to its source literature and the run that produced its data.
A deterministic protocol validator (resource limits, dual-use/biosafety rules) gates physical actions; the model cannot override it.
No physical experiment runs without explicit human authorization (dry-run vs wet-lab tiering).
Dual-use / biosafety hazards are blocked before authorization.
Auditable: a complete lab-notebook record of sources, hypotheses, protocols, authorizations, runs, and results.

capacity.label: Physical runs
capacity.note: Any reagent/robot/biosafety action requires explicit authorization; dry-run analysis is free.

capacity.label: Validation
capacity.note: A protocol validator with dual-use/biosafety rules gates execution; the model cannot override it.

capacity.label: Reproducibility
capacity.note: Every claim links to its source and every datum to the run that produced it.

api.description: Propose an experimental protocol from a hypothesis; it is validated and queued for authorization — it does not run.
A researcher (and biosafety officer if required) authorizes a physical run; only then does lab automation execute.
Fetch the provenance of a finding: source literature for each claim and the run that produced each datum.

dataModel.name: protocols
dataModel.note: A proposed experimental protocol awaiting authorization.

dataModel.name: runs
dataModel.note: An execution of a protocol with its results.

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: An authored DAG (retrieve -> hypothesize -> design -> validate -> authorize -> run), not an autonomous loop driving the lab.
patterns.whenToUse: Where an action drives physical instruments.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A human authorizes any physical experiment; the agent never actuates the lab on its own.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: The agent reasons and designs freely but a validator and authorization gate fence physical execution.
patterns.whenToUse: Design-and-dry-run autonomy with a wet-lab boundary.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground hypotheses in primary literature and prior experimental data.
patterns.whenToUse: Every claim must cite a source.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped access; a wet-lab authorization is bound to a researcher / biosafety principal.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat papers and datasets as untrusted; partition from lab-actuation authority.
patterns.whenToUse: Literature and data are attacker-reachable input.

patterns.name: Durable execution / checkpointing (resume after crash)
patterns.what: Long experiment runs resume exactly-once and never silently re-actuate.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Runs Experiments Autonomously (the baseline)
description: Start with the autonomous-scientist demo: an agent forms a hypothesis and drives the lab robot to test it, end to end, on its own.

Two distinct dangers appear. Physical: an unsupervised agent actuating instruments can consume reagents, damage equipment, or run something with biosafety/dual-use implications — irreversible in the physical world. Scientific: claims drawn from the model with no provenance aren't reproducible, which is its own kind of failure for research.
The rest of the interview turns this into a grounded, validated, human-authorized, reproducible pipeline with a wet-lab gate.

view.caption: An agent designs and runs experiments autonomously — uncontrolled physical actuation and unreproducible claims.

steps.decisionPrompt: What two different kinds of irreversibility does an autonomous lab agent risk?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: The naive autonomous run makes the wet-lab gate and reproducibility requirements concrete.

recap.after: An agent can hypothesize and run an experiment.
recap.newRisk: Uncontrolled physical actuation (reagents, biosafety) and claims with no provenance or reproducibility.

traps.trap: Letting the agent actuate the lab autonomously because it can.
traps.why: Physical actions are irreversible and can be hazardous; claims without provenance aren't science.
traps.instead: Ground and cite, validate the protocol, require human authorization for any physical run, and track provenance.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Primary Literature + Prior Data
description: The grounding corpus is the science: primary literature and prior experimental data. Hypotheses and protocols must be grounded in cited sources and existing results, not in the model's recollection.
Every claim carries a citation from the start, which is what makes downstream reproducibility possible; papers and datasets are treated as untrusted input.

view.caption: Hypotheses and protocols are grounded in cited primary literature and prior experimental data.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why must every claim carry a citation from the moment it's generated?

concepts.definition: Generating each hypothesis/claim with a citation to primary literature or prior data.
concepts.whyItMatters: Ungrounded claims can't be verified or reproduced; provenance has to start at generation.
concepts.example: A proposed mechanism cites the paper and the dataset that motivated it.

whyNow: Grounded, cited claims are the basis for the provenance and reproducibility built later.

recap.after: Hypotheses are grounded in cited literature and data.
recap.newRisk: A grounded protocol can still be unsafe or malformed to execute physically.

steps.title: 3. Deterministic Protocol Validation
description: Before anything physical happens, a deterministic validator checks the proposed protocol: it is well-formed, within resource limits, and clears dual-use / biosafety rules. These checks are hard rules the model cannot override.
The LLM is confined to bounded nodes (literature review, hypothesis, protocol drafting); the validation that decides whether a protocol may even be authorized is deterministic and auditable.

view.caption: A deterministic validator checks protocol well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety rules before authorization.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which protocol checks must be deterministic and un-overridable by the model?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)

concepts.definition: Rule-based checks (well-formedness, resource limits, biosafety/dual-use) applied to a protocol independently of the model.
concepts.whyItMatters: Safety-critical gating of physical actions can't ride on a probabilistic model that can be wrong or injected.
concepts.example: A protocol exceeding a reagent or containment limit is blocked before it ever reaches authorization.

whyNow: Deterministic validation is the safety floor beneath the human authorization gate.

recap.before: A protocol could go straight from the model to the lab.
recap.after: Protocols are deterministically validated and hazardous ones blocked.
recap.newRisk: A valid protocol still must not run physically without a human authorizing it.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Human Authorization for Any Physical Run
description: This is the crux and the new gate for the series: a wet-lab boundary. A validated protocol is queued for authorization, and any action that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or has biosafety implications requires explicit human sign-off — a researcher, plus a biosafety officer when the protocol warrants it. Identity binds the authorization to that principal.
Tiering matters: dry-run analysis and simulation are free; only wet-lab execution needs authorization. The agent designs and analyzes autonomously but never actuates the physical world on its own.

view.caption: Any physical action (reagents, lab robot, biosafety) requires human authorization; dry-runs are free, wet-lab runs are gated.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which actions are free for the agent, and which require human authorization?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: The line between free dry-run analysis and physical execution that consumes reagents or drives instruments, which requires human authorization.
concepts.whyItMatters: Physical actions are irreversible and potentially hazardous; the boundary is where autonomy must stop.
concepts.example: Simulating a reaction is free; running it on the bench needs a researcher's authorization.

whyNow: The wet-lab gate is what makes an autonomous research agent safe to connect to real instruments.

recap.before: The agent could actuate the lab autonomously.
recap.after: Physical runs require human authorization; dry-runs stay free.
recap.newRisk: Results and claims must be reproducible and fully traceable to be science.

steps.title: 5. Reproducibility & Provenance
description: Make the output science, not just text. A provenance store maps every claim to its source literature and every datum to the run that produced it, so a finding can be traced end to end and an experiment re-run from its recorded protocol and conditions.
The audit record doubles as a complete lab notebook: sources, hypotheses, protocols, validations, authorizations, runs, and results, all append-only.

view.caption: Every claim traces to its source and every datum to its run; the audit record is a complete, append-only lab notebook.

steps.decisionPrompt: What must be recorded so another scientist can reproduce a finding?

concepts.definition: A traceable chain from each claim to its source and each datum to the run, protocol, and conditions that produced it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reproducibility is the core scientific requirement; without provenance a result can't be trusted or rebuilt.
concepts.example: A finding links to its source papers and to run #42 with its exact protocol version and parameters.

whyNow: Provenance and reproducibility are what make the platform's output usable as science.

deepDives.title: The lab notebook as audit
points: Append-only record of every source, protocol, authorization, and run.
Each datum carries the run, protocol version, and conditions that produced it.
Raw model traces are stored separately under data governance.

recap.after: Every claim and datum is traceable and reproducible.
recap.newRisk: Hazardous or dual-use protocols must be screened, and scientific reliability measured.

steps.title: 6. Dual-Use & Biosafety Screening
description: Some experiments must never run regardless of who asks. Dual-use and biosafety screening — part of the deterministic validator and the authorization gate — blocks hazardous protocols before authorization, and routes anything with biosafety implications to a biosafety officer.
This is the un-overridable safety floor for the physical world: a protocol the screening flags is stopped no matter how the agent (or an injected instruction in a paper) argues for it.

view.caption: Dual-use/biosafety screening blocks hazardous protocols before authorization and routes biosafety cases to an officer.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you stop an injected instruction in a paper from talking the agent into a hazardous protocol?

concepts.definition: Deterministic checks that block hazardous or dual-use protocols and route biosafety-relevant ones to an officer, independent of the model.
concepts.whyItMatters: The worst physical outcomes must be unreachable even if the model is wrong or manipulated.
concepts.example: A protocol matching a hazard rule is blocked and cannot be authorized through the normal path.

whyNow: Biosafety screening is the hard floor that makes physical autonomy acceptable at all.

recap.before: Hazard depended on the model behaving.
recap.after: Dual-use/biosafety hazards are blocked deterministically and routed to an officer.
recap.newRisk: Scientific reliability across workflows still needs to be measured.

steps.title: 7. Workflows & Scientific-Reliability Evaluation
description: Support the real workflows on this substrate — literature review, hypothesis generation, experimental design, and the design-run-analyze loop — and evaluate what matters for science: claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results, not just whether an answer sounds plausible.
A confident but unsupported or irreproducible claim is a failure here; the eval weights grounding and replication, and watches the rate of blocked/abstained hazardous proposals.

view.caption: Research workflows run on the pipeline; eval weights claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is a plausible-sounding result the wrong thing to optimize for in research?

concepts.definition: Measuring whether claims are grounded and findings replicate, not just whether outputs are fluent or plausible.
concepts.whyItMatters: Plausibility is not truth; only grounding and replication establish a reliable result.
concepts.example: Track claim-grounding rate and how often a finding replicates on re-run.

whyNow: Reliability-weighted evaluation is what keeps the platform scientifically trustworthy at scale.

recap.after: Grounding and reproducibility are evaluated against replicated results.
recap.newRisk: This completes phase 3 of the series: Foundations plus verticals spanning the full gradient and the real-time, contestability, and physical-experiment axes.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Research Platform
finalDesign.description: A research goal enters through the API. A deterministic research pipeline has a design agent review primary literature and prior data and draft a hypothesis and protocol with citations in bounded LLM nodes. A deterministic validator checks the protocol for well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety hazards — blocking hazardous ones — and the model cannot override it. Validated protocols are queued for authorization: any wet-lab action that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or has biosafety implications requires a researcher's (and, when warranted, a biosafety officer's) explicit sign-off bound to that principal, while dry-run analysis is free. Authorized protocols run through lab automation into the data store. A provenance store maps every claim to its source and every datum to its run, the audit record is a complete append-only lab notebook, and evaluation weights claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results. Grounding, durable execution, identity, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; uniquely, the gate guards a physical, irreversible experiment.

functional.how: Grounded design agent over the literature index and data store.
Bounded LLM design node with citations.
Wet-lab authorization gate bound to a researcher/biosafety principal.
Lab automation executes and captures results.

nonFunctional.how: Provenance from claim to source and datum to run.
Wet-lab gate with dry-run vs wet-lab tiering.
Append-only record of sources, protocols, authorizations, runs.

say: Frame the new gate: a physical wet-lab boundary, plus reproducibility as first-class.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the autonomous lab agent and name the two irreversibilities: physical and scientific.
Ground and cite claims, then deterministic protocol validation with biosafety rules.
Human authorization for any physical run (dry-run vs wet-lab), then provenance and reproducibility.
Dual-use/biosafety screening and reproducibility-weighted eval, not plausibility.

expectations: Agent reviews literature and proposes a protocol.
Knows a human authorizes physical runs.
Cited grounding and a deterministic protocol validator.
Wet-lab authorization gate; provenance for findings.
Tiers dry-run vs wet-lab and puts biosafety screening in an un-overridable layer.
Evaluates grounding and replication, not plausibility; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you tier actions into free dry-runs vs gated wet-lab execution?
What must be recorded for a finding to be independently reproducible?
How do you keep dual-use/biosafety screening robust against injected instructions in a paper?
How does the physical-experiment gate compare to the healthcare and finance gates in the series?
